Demo Day and Your Service Fell Asleep? Use UptimeRobot or cron-job.org to Keep It Awake

As a dev, knocking out a side project after work is pretty normal. We usually park these on free hosting platforms — but a lot of those platforms have the same gotcha: if no one's using the service, it goes to sleep, and the next visitor has to wait for it to wake up. So this post covers two services for keeping a free-tier service alive — UptimeRobot and cron-job.org.
As mentioned — small projects and toys usually live on free hosting.
But every free tier has limits. From

Web Services on the free instance type are automatically spun down after 15 minutes of inactivity. When a new request for a free service comes in, Render spins it up again so it can process the request.
At this point you might be thinking:
"Why not just skip Render.com? Use fly.io or Vercel?"

True — if responsiveness matters and you can't tolerate cold-start latency, switching is the cleaner move. That said, I personally like Render.com because its UI is simpler than fly.io's or Vercel's, and it feels close to old Heroku (Heroku killed its free tier on 2022/11/28).
So if your platform has an idle-sleep policy, what do you do? Easy — if the server is being used, it doesn't sleep. So just make sure it's always being used.
How? There are free services that handle this for you:
cronhub is only free for 7 days, then it's paid — so I'm skipping it.
— your service URL. If it's on
— on the free tier the minimum is 5 minutes, so pick 5 minutes
— how long until it's considered down. Default 30 seconds is fine
Leave everything else as default and click
. (If you didn't pick anything for Alert Contacts To Notify you'll see a warning — ignore it.) The new monitor shows up in the dashboard.
Now UptimeRobot pings your service every 5 minutes, and it won't fall asleep.
Render.com's docs say sleep kicks in at 15 minutes idle, so a 5-minute ping interval has plenty of margin.
You also get uptime stats from the monitor — 100% means no downtime during the monitoring window, 99.9% means 0.1% downtime.
This part is honestly the side benefit I use most — if your service goes down, you get an email about it, and you can go fix it right away.
cron-job.org is more of a pure scheduler than a monitor — it fires scheduled HTTP requests, which incidentally keeps your service awake.
Setup is just as easy. Assuming you're already registered, you'll see this:
— your service URL, again something like
The cron job shows up in your dashboard.
That's it — your service gets pinged on schedule, so it never falls asleep.
UptimeRobot and cron-job.org cover the same need. UptimeRobot has more features so it's the one I personally lean on, but either works.

Side projects on free hosting tiers tend to sleep after periods of inactivity — and the wake-up delay is the worst when you're trying to demo something. This post covers how to keep a free Render.com (or similar) service awake using UptimeRobot or cron-job.org, with the full setup walkthrough for both — what URL to monitor, what interval to pick, and which one I personally end up using.
